core_orientation: Digitally confident, health-literate users who actively manage their own care, expect modern digital experiences as baseline, and are frustrated by the gap between what is technically possible and what the healthcare system currently offers.
key_jobs: Track claim status in real time (JOB-001); Act on problems directly through the portal (JOB-003); Get proactive push notifications (JOB-006); Integrate portal with other health tools (JOB-010); See personalized experience for chronic patients (JOB-010)
primary_pains: Portal is view-only (PAIN-003); Medication changes without context (PAIN-001); Health systems do not interoperate (PAIN-002); No deductible visibility (PAIN-009)
primary_gains: Centralized dashboard replaces manual tracking (GAIN-002); Time savings from eliminated calls (GAIN-006); Brand confidence in insurer (GAIN-007); Real-time status as standard (GAIN-001)

core_orientation: Users who want to manage their own affairs independently but face significant barriers in digital confidence, health literacy, and jargon comprehension. They experience a painful gap between desire and capability.
key_jobs: Understand health admin in plain language (JOB-005); Feel competent and independent (JOB-007); Access human co-browse support (GAIN-005); Practice in safe environment (JOB-011); See claim status without calling (JOB-001)
primary_pains: DigiD login blocks access (PAIN-005); Insurance jargon triggers anxiety (PAIN-004); Fear of irreversible mistakes (PAIN-011); Medication changes cause panic (PAIN-001); Dependence on family erodes self-worth
primary_gains: Restored independence and self-reliance (GAIN-003); Claim status without calling (GAIN-001); Phone support with co-browsing (GAIN-005); Learning at own pace with patience

core_orientation: Time-poor users managing health administration for multiple family members alongside their own needs. Their core challenge is fragmentation across systems, identities, and people's needs in stolen moments.
key_jobs: Manage multiple family members in one dashboard (JOB-004); Receive notifications for dependents (JOB-006/JOB-009); Complete tasks quickly in short windows; Track all claims across family (JOB-001); Ensure formal authorization reflected in system (JOB-009)
primary_pains: No centralized multi-person view (PAIN-006); Medication changes communicated to wrong person (PAIN-001); Admin competes with caregiving for time; Dependent DigiD physically inaccessible (PAIN-005); Accidental cross-polis submissions
primary_gains: Profile-switching dashboard -- Netflix model (GAIN-002); Time converted to quality time (GAIN-006); Proactive notifications for dependents (GAIN-004); Quick check-in interactions fitting fragmented time

core_orientation: Users whose trust in digital health systems is conditional on granular privacy controls. They distinguish between legal compliance and felt safety, and evaluate every feature through the lens of data exposure risk.
key_jobs: Control visibility of sensitive health categories (JOB-008); Know who accessed my data (JOB-014); Ensure notifications do not reveal health info (JOB-006 tension); Dispute medication changes (JOB-003); Understand data retention policy
primary_pains: Centralized data increases vulnerability (PAIN-007); Psychotropic medication switching has unacknowledged effects (PAIN-001); No way to challenge medication switch (PAIN-003); GGZ visible in claim overviews; Search queries may be logged
primary_gains: Granular privacy controls as first-class feature; Audit log of data access; Selective feature use while hiding sensitive categories; Feeling heard in medication decisions (GAIN-003)
